sections.title: Overview
sections.content: A burn is tissue damage that is caused by exposure to heat, cold, chemicals, electricity, friction, or radiation. The severity of a burn is determined by the depth and the extent of the body surface area affected. They range from minor to life-threatening and can result in significant fluid loss, infection, and organ dysfunction.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Assess airway burns for (airway swelling may develop hours after injury):
  - Soot in nasal and mouth cavities, cough and hoarseness, black sputum, difficulty in breathing and swallowing
  - Blistering around mouth and tongue, scorched hair, eyebrows or facial hair
  - Pain, pallor, respiratory distress, hypoxia, oedema, anxiety

 **Depth Assessment**
  **- Superficial(First Degree):**
   - Involves the epithelium
  - Pink, Red
  - Painful
  - No Blisters

  **- Superficial Partial(Second Degree):**
   - Epidermis and superficial dermis destroyed
   - Very Painful
   - Often Blistered
   - Pink, moist
   - Blanches

  **- Deep Partial(Second Degree):**
   - Involves epidermis and reticular dermis
  - May blister
  - Mottled pink & white
  - Fairly dry after a day or two
  - Discomfort rather than pain
  - Slow or no cap refill

  **- Full Thickness(Third Degree):**
   - Involves epidermis, dermis, may include fat
  - Does not blanch
  - May be mottled, dry, translucent, black or pale in appearance
  - Full thickness scalds may have a non-blanching appearance

 **Healing & Scarring**
 **- Superficial (First Degree):**
   - Complete, scarless healing within 7 days

 **- Superficial Partial (second Degree):**
   - Heals by epithelial migration within 2 weeks
  - Can convert to deeper injury

 **- Deep Partial (Second Degree):**
   - Will take more than 3 weeks to heal

 **- Full Thickness (Third Degree):**
   - Will not heal, unless very small.
  - Scarring ++
  - Early excision and grafting will reduce scarring and contracture

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Assess burn depth, TBSA, inhalation injury, special sites, circumferential burns, and associated trauma
- Burns involving the face, hands, feet, genitalia, major joints, or circumferential chest or limb burns require burns-service review
- Cooling should avoid hypothermia; do not use ice
- Enclosed-space burns should raise concern for inhalation injury
- Hydrofluoric acid burns require hospital transfer and specific treatment

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey with c-spine consideration where mechanism suggests it
- Reassess the airway frequently and support ventilation if required
- Administer oxygen if indicated
- Cool the burn with cool running water for 20 minutes if within 3 hours of injury while keeping the patient warm
- Remove jewellery and constricting clothing unless adhered to the wound
- Cover the burn after cooling with cling film or a clean dry dressing
- Record vital signs, assess TBSA and depth, and complete focused secondary survey
- Expedite referral when burns-service criteria are met

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Prepare for advanced airway management if inhalation injury is suspected
- Establish vascular access through intact skin where possible
- Begin fluid resuscitation for burns ≥15% TBSA (≥10% TBSA in children/elderly):

**Parkland Formula:** 4 mL × %TBSA × weight (kg) of crystalloid in first 24 hours
- Give 50% in the first 8 hours from **time of burn** (not time of arrival)
- Give remaining 50% over the following 16 hours
- Use cool/tepid IV fluid if available; apply MEQU warmer for paediatric and hypothermic patients

- Provide analgesia and antiemetic therapy within scope
- Cardiac monitoring where indicated
- Do not routinely debride burns or deroof blisters in the prehospital setting
- Keep patient warm — burn patients are at high risk of hypothermia

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Compartment syndrome; signs of ischaemia include:
   - Pain
   - Pallor
   - Paraesthesia
   - Pulselessness or >2 seconds CRT
   - Cool or cold limb
- Referral criteria to state Adult Burn Unit at FSH:
   - Burns greater than 10% total body surface area, full thickness > 5% TBSA
   - Circumferential partial thickness or full thickness burns
   - Burns in conjunction with major trauma
   - Inhalation burns
   - Chemical burns
   - Electrical burns
  - Special area burns i.e. face, neck, hands, feet, perineum/genitalia, joints
